story:
Pets were the furthest things from Vera’s mind as she performed her seasonal ritual, swapping winter for summer in the hall closet, when a basement bellow from her ten-year-old cut through the house. Mothers know the difference between pain and discovery; this was discovery. She squirmed out of the closet, scolded Chip the brain-light chocolate Lab for the nose to her backside, and shuffled to the stairs. From below came excited words: “cat,” “froze,” “gross.” In a flash of maternal dread, Vera pictured it. “Peabody!”

“There’s something that looks like a cat in the freezer,” Harry called. “It’s all dead-looking,” seven-year-old Maisie added. Vera’s heart lurched. Why hadn’t she carried out the plan?

Twelve months earlier, a pale pink brick had fallen from the peak of their new “Heritage Acres” house—a huge, flimsy castle on a bathmat-sized lot—and landed on Mrs. Pudley, the Melvins’ adored cat. Relations with the Melvins had already soured the day Chip, high on mud and opportunity, rampaged through their just-moved-in home, turning floors, stairs, and a prized Niagara Falls pillow into a map of pawprints. Apologies hadn’t helped. The brick sealed it. Vera had brought the wrapped remains to Megan Melvin, who wailed like a siren. Word later had it the Belangers were “dangerous sociopaths.”

Repairs were done; promises were made. Then, that autumn, another piece of house came down, this time onto the Belangers’ own cat. Chip snorted dust at the patio scene; Vera’s stomach fell. Peabody had been around since before Harry was born. How to tell the children?

Vera concocted what felt, then, like mercy: “flash-freeze” Peabody in the deep freezer’s hollow by the motor, wrapped in layers of plastic and sealed beneath a piece of plywood, then cover him with entrées and Christmas baking like a culinary headstone. When winter truly set in, Paul would quietly place him at the curb. Meanwhile, the story would be that Peabody had wandered off to a new, tuna-rich family. The children made Missing Cat posters, ran to check messages after school, and finally, with small wounded hearts, let him go. And then winter passed, and the plan—cowardly and unnecessary, as time would show—was never finished.

“Why’s there a bag with a cat in it in the freezer? And why’s it so flat?” Harry asked now. The truth, heavy as that brick, left Vera no room for another tale. She sat them at the kitchen table, poured juice, and confessed—about the shoddy house, Mrs. Pudley, and Peabody’s death. She admitted the lie and the freezer and apologized. The children listened, solemn and clear-eyed.

“This house is a cat-killer,” Maisie declared. Harry, practical: “We could have just buried him, Mom.” And so they did. When Paul arrived, he and Harry dug by the fence. Maisie brought a stone lettered “Peabody.” They laid him down, and the children offered a small eulogy: twelve good years, profession house pet, hobbies squirrels and field mice, favorite trick riding Chip’s back and vanishing under furniture. There were tears and chuckles. Paul grilled tuna—Peabody’s favorite—and they told stories until the light went soft.

That night, Vera wept a little for the lie, and for how quickly the children forgave it. Paul squeezed her hand. “They’re more resilient than we give them credit for,” he said. Vera nodded, thinking of the house that dropped bricks like curses. “Maybe we should sell,” she murmured. “Something downtown. Older. With charm. Maybe even needing work. I don’t trust this house around pets.”
